tagged_story_for_tts:
Shiny metal.<[silence:700ms]> Unreactive treasure.<[silence:700ms]>

The word gold comes from Old English geolu, meaning "yellow"—the same root as yolk—because nothing else glows like it without tarnishing.<[silence:700ms]> Gold doesn’t rust, corrode, or dissolve in most acids;<[silence:100ms]> even buried for millennia, it emerges untouched.<[silence:700ms]> This stubborn purity made it the first global currency:<[silence:120ms]> 2,600 years ago, King Croesus of Lydia stamped gold nuggets into coins, replacing barter with portable wealth.<[silence:700ms]>

Gold’s density is deceptive.<[silence:700ms]> A cubic foot weighs as much as a small car (1,200 pounds), yet it’s soft enough to dent with a fingernail—ancient Egyptians hammered it into leaf thinner than paper for pharaohs’ death masks.<[silence:700ms]> Today, a single ounce (the size of a golf ball) can be stretched into a 50-mile wire, used in phones and satellites because it conducts electricity better than copper.<[silence:700ms]>

Most gold on Earth arrived via asteroid bombardments 4 billion years ago, sinking into the planet’s core.<[silence:700ms]> The gold we mine?<[silence:150ms]> Leftovers, scattered in veins by hydrothermal fluids—like a geologic sieve concentrating flecks over eons.<[silence:700ms]>

Three related subjects are alchemical obsessions, deep-sea mineral deposits, and how metals shape economies.<[silence:700ms]>
clean_story:
Shiny metal. Unreactive treasure.

The word gold comes from Old English geolu, meaning "yellow"—the same root as yolk—because nothing else glows like it without tarnishing. Gold doesn’t rust, corrode, or dissolve in most acids; even buried for millennia, it emerges untouched. This stubborn purity made it the first global currency: 2,600 years ago, King Croesus of Lydia stamped gold nuggets into coins, replacing barter with portable wealth.

Gold’s density is deceptive. A cubic foot weighs as much as a small car (1,200 pounds), yet it’s soft enough to dent with a fingernail—ancient Egyptians hammered it into leaf thinner than paper for pharaohs’ death masks. Today, a single ounce (the size of a golf ball) can be stretched into a 50-mile wire, used in phones and satellites because it conducts electricity better than copper.

Most gold on Earth arrived via asteroid bombardments 4 billion years ago, sinking into the planet’s core. The gold we mine? Leftovers, scattered in veins by hydrothermal fluids—like a geologic sieve concentrating flecks over eons.

Three related subjects are alchemical obsessions, deep-sea mineral deposits, and how metals shape economies.
